scenario:
A company runs a three-tier web application across two AWS accounts within the same AWS Organization: a Shared Services account hosting a fleet of Network Load Balancers behind a central inspection VPC, and a Workload account hosting the application in a separate VPC. The security team requires that:

- Traffic from the Workload VPC to the Shared Services VPC must never traverse the public internet.
- The Shared Services team must be able to add or remove consuming accounts without modifying route tables in every workload VPC.
- The solution must scale to support hundreds of workload VPCs across many accounts in the future without hitting AWS's default VPC peering connection limits per VPC.

Which solution meets these requirements with the least ongoing operational overhead?

options.text: Create a VPC Peering connection between the Shared Services VPC and each Workload VPC. Update route tables in every VPC to route traffic to the peered CIDR ranges.
Create a Transit Gateway, attach every VPC (Shared Services and all Workload VPCs) to it, and configure Transit Gateway route tables to control which VPCs can reach the Shared Services VPC.
Deploy an AWS PrivateLink endpoint service in the Shared Services account fronted by the Network Load Balancers, and create VPC interface endpoints in each Workload VPC that consume the service.
Configure AWS Site-to-Site VPN connections between the Shared Services VPC and each Workload VPC using a Virtual Private Gateway.

explanation.correct: AWS PrivateLink is purpose-built for this exact pattern — a service provider (Shared Services, fronted by an NLB) exposing a service to many consumer VPCs/accounts without any VPC peering, route table changes, or CIDR overlap concerns. Adding a new consuming account is just a matter of accepting a new endpoint connection — the provider never touches route tables, and there's no limit tied to the number of consumers the way peering has a per-VPC peering connection soft limit (125 by default, though raisable). Traffic stays entirely on the AWS network backbone, never touching the public internet.

incorrect.A: VPC Peering doesn't scale here — peering is strictly 1:1 between VPCs, has no transitive routing, and each new workload VPC requires both a new peering connection AND route table updates in every VPC involved. This directly violates the "hundreds of VPCs" and "least operational overhead" requirements.
incorrect.B: A very plausible-looking distractor, and often the right answer for full network-layer connectivity between many VPCs — but it's the wrong tool here because it grants full network reachability (not just access to specific NLB-fronted services), and still requires the Shared Services team to manage Transit Gateway route table associations/propagations for every new attachment. PrivateLink better matches "expose one service, don't manage per-consumer routing."
incorrect.D: Site-to-Site VPN is designed for connecting to on-premises networks, not VPC-to-VPC connectivity within AWS. It adds unnecessary encryption/tunnel overhead and per-connection management, with no advantage over PrivateLink or Transit Gateway for intra-AWS traffic.

scenario:
A central platform team manages networking for a large enterprise with 40 application teams, each operating its own AWS account under the same AWS Organization. Requirements:

- All application teams should deploy resources into a shared pool of subnets managed centrally, to avoid IP address exhaustion and inconsistent CIDR planning across 40 separate VPCs.
- Application teams must be able to launch EC2 instances, RDS databases, and other resources into these shared subnets from their own accounts.
- Application teams must NOT be able to modify the shared VPC's route tables, security group defaults, or subnet configuration — only the central platform team should control those.
- The solution should minimize the number of VPCs, cross-VPC connections, and peering relationships the platform team has to manage.

Which approach satisfies these requirements?

options.text: Create a VPC per application team, and use AWS Transit Gateway to connect all 40 VPCs together with a shared services VPC containing common resources.

Use VPC sharing (via AWS Resource Access Manager) to share subnets from a single central VPC owned by the platform team's account with each of the 40 application team accounts as participants, allowing each team to launch resources into the shared subnets while the platform team retains ownership of the VPC, route tables, and NACLs.
Create a VPC per application team and use VPC peering between each application team's VPC and a shared services VPC to centralize IP address management.
Grant each application team's IAM role a resource-based policy to create resources directly inside the platform team's VPC, and rely on IAM policies alone to prevent route table modification.

explanation.correct: VPC sharing (RAM-shared subnets) is the exact AWS feature for this scenario: a single owner VPC's subnets are shared with participant accounts, which can launch resources (EC2, RDS, etc.) into those subnets using their own account's billing and resource ownership — but participants can never modify the VPC's route tables, NACLs, security group defaults, gateways, or peering/attachment configuration, since only the owning account controls those. This collapses 40 potential VPCs into one, directly minimizing CIDR planning overhead and eliminating the need for any peering or Transit Gateway attachments between teams.

incorrect.A: 40 separate VPCs plus a Transit Gateway still means 40 CIDR ranges to plan and 40 attachments to manage — it doesn't minimize VPC count or avoid CIDR exhaustion planning. Each team also still fully owns and can modify its own VPC's route tables, which fails the "must not modify" requirement.
incorrect.C: Full mesh-style peering to a shared services VPC still requires 40 separate VPCs and up to 40 peering connections, directly contradicting the "minimize VPCs and connections" requirement, and again each team retains full control over its own VPC.
incorrect.D: IAM resource-based policies aren't how cross-account subnet access is granted in AWS — VPC sharing via RAM is the actual mechanism. Relying on IAM policies alone to prevent infrastructure changes is weaker and not how AWS implements this boundary.

scenario:
A global company has 10 VPCs in us-east-1 and 8 VPCs in eu-west-1, plus an on-premises data center connected via Direct Connect to us-east-1. Requirements:

- All VPCs within each Region must be able to communicate with each other and with the on-premises network.
- Three specific VPCs in us-east-1 hold PCI workloads (the "Sensitive" segment) and must NOT be reachable from any eu-west-1 VPC, even though all VPCs will attach to Transit Gateways.
- Cross-Region traffic between the two Regions' Transit Gateways must never traverse the public internet.

Which combination of actions meets these requirements?

options.text: Create a single Transit Gateway in us-east-1, attach the eu-west-1 VPCs to it via VPC peering, and use one Transit Gateway route table for all attachments.

Create a Transit Gateway in each Region, connect them with Transit Gateway peering, and use multiple Transit Gateway route tables per Region — associating the Sensitive VPC attachments with a dedicated route table that does not have routes propagated to or from the eu-west-1 attachments.
Create a Transit Gateway in each Region, connect them with a Site-to-Site VPN over the internet, and rely on Security Groups alone in the Sensitive VPCs to block traffic from eu-west-1.
Create separate VPC peering connections between every pair of VPCs across both Regions and use NACLs to block traffic from eu-west-1 into the Sensitive VPCs.

explanation.correct: Transit Gateway peering connects two regional Transit Gateways privately over the AWS backbone, satisfying "never traverse the public internet." Network segmentation is achieved with multiple Transit Gateway route tables: associating the Sensitive VPCs' attachments with a route table that doesn't propagate routes to/from the eu-west-1 attachments isolates them at the routing layer, while all other same-Region VPCs and the on-premises network continue to communicate normally through their own associated/propagated route tables. This is the standard Transit Gateway network-segmentation pattern.

incorrect.A: Transit Gateway doesn't span Regions this way — cross-Region connectivity requires Transit Gateway peering between two regional Transit Gateways, not plain VPC peering directly to a Transit Gateway. A single shared route table also provides no segmentation at all.
incorrect.C: A Site-to-Site VPN over the internet directly violates the "must never traverse the public internet" requirement. Security Groups alone also don't provide network-level route isolation — they filter traffic to/from resources that are still routable, which is a weaker and less reliable segmentation boundary than route table isolation.
incorrect.D: A full mesh of VPC peering connections across 18 VPCs doesn't scale (no transitive routing, and a rapidly growing number of pairwise connections to manage), and NACLs are a much higher-maintenance, per-subnet mechanism compared to centralized route-table-based segmentation at the Transit Gateway.

scenario:
A financial services company has a single AWS Direct Connect connection from its data center to a Direct Connect location, terminating in a private VIF on a Virtual Private Gateway. Requirements:

- Loss of any single Direct Connect location or device must not cause a full connectivity outage.
- The design should follow an AWS-documented resiliency model that eliminates single points of failure in the Direct Connect path.
- If all Direct Connect paths fail simultaneously, traffic must automatically fail over to an encrypted path over the internet, with no manual intervention required.

Which combination of steps satisfies these requirements?

options.text: Provision a second Direct Connect connection at the same Direct Connect location, using the same device and cross-connect, and configure equal BGP priority on both.

Following the AWS Direct Connect Resiliency Toolkit, provision two Direct Connect connections at two different Direct Connect locations terminating on different devices, and configure a Site-to-Site VPN as a backup path with a less-preferred BGP path (e.g. longer AS_PATH) than the Direct Connect routes.
Use a single Direct Connect connection with a Link Aggregation Group (LAG) to increase throughput, and rely on Direct Connect's built-in automatic failover to the internet.

Provision two Direct Connect connections at two different Direct Connect locations behind a Direct Connect Gateway and Transit Gateway, and omit a VPN backup since the Direct Connect Gateway is redundant by default.

explanation.correct: The Direct Connect Resiliency Toolkit's "High Resiliency" model (and above) requires connections at multiple Direct Connect locations terminating on separate devices, eliminating both location- and device-level single points of failure. Layering a Site-to-Site VPN backup path with a deliberately less-preferred BGP route (longer AS_PATH or lower local preference relative to the Direct Connect routes) means BGP will naturally prefer Direct Connect when available and automatically fail over to the encrypted VPN path if all Direct Connect routes are withdrawn — with no manual re-routing needed.

incorrect.A: Using the same location and device provides no protection against a location- or device-level failure — both connections share the same failure domain, which the Resiliency Toolkit explicitly guards against.
incorrect.C: Direct Connect has no built-in automatic failover to the internet — a VPN or a second Direct Connect connection must be explicitly provisioned and configured as backup. LAG only aggregates bandwidth and redundancy of links to the same location, not against a location-level outage.
incorrect.D: A Direct Connect Gateway is a routing construct for connecting Direct Connect connections to VPCs across regions/accounts — it does not itself provide a backup connectivity path. Omitting the VPN backup leaves no failover option if both Direct Connect connections fail at once (e.g., a regional Direct Connect service disruption), violating the automatic-internet-failover requirement.

scenario: A company connects an on-premises data center to a VPC using a single AWS Site-to-Site VPN connection (two tunnels, active/standby). The on-premises-to-AWS application needs sustained throughput of 3 Gbps, but each VPN tunnel is capped at roughly 1.25 Gbps. The company also wants the design to scale to connect dozens of on-premises sites to hundreds of VPCs in the future without significant added operational complexity, and cannot provision Direct Connect due to a short project timeline. What should a solutions architect recommend?

options.text: Increase the customer gateway device to a larger EC2 instance type to raise the per-tunnel throughput beyond 1.25 Gbps.

Provision multiple Site-to-Site VPN connections to a Transit Gateway and enable Equal Cost Multi-Path (ECMP) routing across the tunnels to aggregate bandwidth, using the Transit Gateway as the hub for future on-premises sites and VPCs.
Replace the Site-to-Site VPN with a single AWS Client VPN endpoint, which supports higher per-connection throughput than Site-to-Site VPN.
Enable Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN on the existing single VPN connection terminating on a Virtual Private Gateway, which removes the per-tunnel throughput cap entirely.

explanation.correct: The ~1.25 Gbps per-tunnel limit is a protocol-level IPsec constraint, not something a larger customer gateway instance can overcome. AWS's documented pattern for exceeding a single tunnel's throughput is multiple VPN connections combined with ECMP routing through a Transit Gateway, which can spread traffic across several tunnels to aggregate bandwidth. A Transit Gateway also naturally scales into a hub-and-spoke model for connecting many future sites and VPCs with minimal added operational complexity.

incorrect.A: The per-tunnel throughput cap is a fixed protocol-level limit of the IPsec tunnel itself, not a function of the customer gateway device's compute size — a larger instance won't raise it.
incorrect.C: AWS Client VPN is designed for individual remote user/device access (an OpenVPN-based solution), not site-to-site network connectivity, and isn't a throughput-superior substitute for this use case.
incorrect.D: Accelerated Site-to-Site VPN uses AWS Global Accelerator to improve routing and connection reliability to the nearest AWS edge location — it does not remove or raise the ~1.25 Gbps per-tunnel throughput cap. Multiple tunnels with ECMP are still required to scale aggregate throughput.

scenario:
A media company runs an application in two AWS Regions (us-east-1 primary, ap-southeast-1 secondary) behind an Application Load Balancer in each Region. Requirements:

- Under normal conditions, users should be routed to whichever Region gives them the lowest network latency.
- If the primary Region's ALB becomes unhealthy, traffic must automatically fail over to the secondary Region, without depending on individual clients' DNS caching behavior to eventually pick up the change.
- The company is also running a canary release in us-east-1 and wants only 5% of us-east-1-directed traffic to reach the canary environment, with the rest going to the stable environment — without altering the latency-based/failover behavior between the two Regions.

How should a solutions architect design the Route 53 record set to satisfy all of these requirements?

options.text: Create two simple A records, one per Region, and have the application redirect canary traffic client-side.

Create latency-based alias records for us-east-1 and ap-southeast-1, each with an associated Route 53 health check so an unhealthy Region is automatically excluded from DNS answers. Under the us-east-1 latency record, use a nested weighted record set (95%/5%) as the alias target to split traffic between the stable and canary target groups.
Create a single weighted record set with the two Regions weighted 50/50, attach a health check to each weighted record, and manually adjust the weights during each canary release to redirect traffic.
Create a failover routing policy with us-east-1 as primary and ap-southeast-1 as secondary, and use a separate CNAME for canary traffic that customers must opt into manually.

explanation.correct: This requires nesting Route 53 routing policies, an advanced but well-documented pattern: a latency-based record set with health checks determines the lowest-latency healthy Region and lets Route 53 itself exclude an unhealthy Region's answers at the authoritative DNS level (rather than depending on client-side caching behavior). Nesting a weighted record set specifically under the us-east-1 latency-based record then splits only that Region's traffic 95/5 between stable and canary, without touching ap-southeast-1's routing at all.

incorrect.A: Simple records provide no latency-based or health-check-driven failover behavior — this fails both the latency-routing and automatic-failover requirements outright.
incorrect.C: A flat 50/50 weighted policy doesn't route by latency (violating the first requirement), and manually adjusting weights for every canary release is exactly the kind of operational overhead a well-architected design should avoid, not require.
incorrect.D: A failover policy satisfies automatic Region failover but abandons latency-based routing entirely under normal conditions (violating the first requirement), and a manual opt-in CNAME doesn't deliver the required automatic 5% canary split.

scenario:
A software company (Provider Co.) wants to offer its internal analytics API as a paid service to external customers, each operating an independent AWS account outside Provider Co.'s AWS Organization. Requirements:

- Customers must reach the API privately from their own VPCs, without traversing the public internet, and Provider Co. must have no visibility into or control over customers' networks.
- Provider Co. must be able to explicitly approve or reject each customer's connection request before it becomes active.
- Customers should reach the API using a friendly, private DNS name (e.g. api.providerco.com) rather than an AWS-generated endpoint DNS name.
- Provider Co.'s infrastructure details (VPC CIDR, instance IPs, etc.) must remain hidden from customers at all times.

Which solution meets all of these requirements?

options.text: Provider Co. creates a VPC peering connection with each customer's VPC and shares route tables so customers can access the API using Provider Co.'s private IP addresses directly.

Provider Co. creates an AWS PrivateLink endpoint service backed by a Network Load Balancer in front of the API, requires manual acceptance for new endpoint connections, and associates a verified private DNS name so customers can resolve api.providerco.com to the interface endpoint inside their own VPC.

Provider Co. publishes the API through a public Amazon API Gateway endpoint secured with API keys, and instructs customers to allow-list Provider Co.'s IP ranges in their own VPC's outbound security group rules.
Provider Co. creates a Transit Gateway and shares it with each customer account via AWS Resource Access Manager, and each customer attaches their VPC to Provider Co.'s Transit Gateway.

explanation.correct: AWS PrivateLink is purpose-built for exactly this cross-organization SaaS-provider pattern: it exposes only the NLB's listening ports to consumers, hiding the provider's VPC CIDR, route tables, and instance IPs entirely; it requires explicit connection acceptance so the provider approves each customer individually; and it supports a verified private DNS name for the endpoint service so customers can use a custom domain instead of the auto-generated vpce-*.amazonaws.com name — all without the provider touching any customer network configuration.

incorrect.A: VPC Peering exposes full network reachability and requires sharing/coordinating route tables between two independent organizations, directly violating "infrastructure details must remain hidden," and it has no per-connection approval workflow at the granularity PrivateLink provides.
incorrect.C: A public API Gateway endpoint traverses the public internet, violating that explicit requirement, and IP allow-listing is brittle and doesn't scale cleanly across many independent customer accounts compared to a private, connection-based network solution.
incorrect.D: Sharing a Transit Gateway via RAM grants full network-layer reachability into all of Provider Co.'s TGW-attached VPCs to any account that attaches — the opposite of hiding infrastructure — and it isn't designed for onboarding untrusted third-party accounts the way PrivateLink's explicit accept/reject model is.

scenario: A company wants to delegate to its DevOps team the ability to create new IAM roles and policies for their applications, but must guarantee that no one — including the DevOps team itself, even by accident — can ever create or escalate a role to administrator-level access. Developers still need to be able to create functional roles for legitimate application services. Which approach achieves this?

options.text: Attach an explicit Deny policy to each DevOps IAM user for iam:CreateRole with a condition matching the AdministratorAccess managed policy ARN.

Set a permissions boundary on the DevOps IAM role that caps the maximum permissions any role or user the DevOps team creates can ever have, and grant DevOps iam:CreateRole/iam:PutRolePolicy permissions only when that boundary is specified.
Use an AWS Config rule to detect any IAM role that has AdministratorAccess attached and automatically delete it on a scheduled basis.
Create a Service Control Policy at the OU level that denies iam:CreateRole entirely, and grant DevOps engineers break-glass root access whenever they need to create a new role.

explanation.correct: Permissions boundaries are IAM's mechanism for capping the maximum permissions a delegated administrator (here, DevOps) can ever grant to identities they create. Even if DevOps attaches an overly broad policy to a new role, the role's effective permissions are the intersection of its policy AND the boundary — so it can never exceed the boundary, regardless of what DevOps grants. This lets DevOps keep creating functional roles for legitimate services while making privilege escalation architecturally impossible rather than merely policy-forbidden.

incorrect.A: An explicit deny targeting one specific managed policy ARN is trivially bypassed — DevOps could attach an inline policy or a different managed policy with equivalent permissions. It blocks one technique, not the underlying capability.
incorrect.C: Detect-and-delete is a reactive, not preventive, control — there's a window where an over-privileged role exists and could already have been used before Config catches and removes it. This fails "guarantee...can ever," which requires prevention.
incorrect.D: Denying iam:CreateRole entirely via SCP blocks DevOps from creating any role at all, including legitimate ones for application services — violating the requirement that developers must still be able to create functional roles. Routine break-glass root access is also a security anti-pattern and heavy operational overhead.

scenario:
A company acquires another company and must bring the acquired company's 15 existing standalone AWS accounts into its AWS Organization. Requirements:

- Consolidated billing must cover all accounts, including the newly acquired ones.
- The acquiring company's existing SCP guardrails must eventually apply to the acquired accounts, but NOT immediately — there needs to be a grace period while each acquired account's workloads are assessed for compliance.
- The acquired accounts' workloads must keep running without service interruption throughout the process.

Which approach satisfies these requirements?

options.text: Have the acquired company close all 15 existing accounts and create brand-new accounts inside the acquiring company's AWS Organization, manually migrating all resources.

Send an invitation from the Organization's management account to each of the 15 existing accounts to join the Organization; place newly joined accounts into a dedicated, temporary OU with no (or minimal) SCPs attached, then move each account into the appropriate production OU with full SCPs once its assessment is complete.
Use AWS Control Tower's Account Factory to enroll the 15 existing accounts, which immediately and unconditionally attaches all guardrails currently applied to other accounts.
Add the 15 accounts to the Organization directly under the root, and rely on IAM policies within each account to temporarily block actions until the account is assessed.

explanation.correct: AWS Organizations lets existing standalone accounts be invited to join an Organization without recreating them or interrupting running workloads. Placing newly invited accounts into a dedicated staging/quarantine OU with no or minimal SCPs — since SCPs are inherited by OU membership — then moving them into production OUs (with full guardrails) once assessed is the standard "safe landing zone for acquired accounts" pattern, cleanly satisfying the grace-period requirement. Consolidated billing applies automatically to every member account the moment it joins, satisfying that requirement immediately.

incorrect.A: Recreating accounts and manually migrating every resource is extremely high-effort, causes service interruption, and is exactly what the account-invitation feature of Organizations exists to avoid.
incorrect.C: Control Tower enrollment of existing accounts does apply guardrails, but doing so "immediately and unconditionally" directly contradicts the explicit grace-period requirement before enforcement.
incorrect.D: SCPs are the correct org-level control for this, not per-account IAM policies — IAM policies can't provide the same organization-wide guardrail enforcement, and manually rigging temporary IAM blocks across 15 separate accounts is far higher operational overhead than simple OU placement.

scenario: A security team wants to guarantee that no IAM principal in any account within a specific OU (containing all production accounts) can ever disable AWS CloudTrail, delete CloudTrail logs, or disable GuardDuty — including the account's own root user or an administrator holding an AdministratorAccess IAM policy. Which approach achieves this guarantee?

options.text: Attach an IAM policy to every administrator role in each production account explicitly denying cloudtrail:StopLogging, cloudtrail:DeleteTrail, and guardduty:DeleteDetector.

Attach a Service Control Policy to the production OU that explicitly denies cloudtrail:StopLogging, cloudtrail:DeleteTrail, and guardduty:DeleteDetector, and ensure no SCP elsewhere in the hierarchy re-allows them.
Enable an AWS Config managed rule that flags CloudTrail or GuardDuty being disabled, and send an SNS notification to the security team when this happens.
Use IAM Access Analyzer to continuously monitor and automatically revoke any permissions related to CloudTrail or GuardDuty modification across all production accounts.

explanation.correct: SCPs are the only control here that applies to every principal in an account — including the root user — and they set a hard ceiling that no IAM policy within the account can ever exceed, no matter how privileged. This is the only option that can guarantee the action is blocked "even for the root user," since IAM policies don't restrict root the same way, and detective controls only report on an action after it has already happened.

incorrect.A: IAM policies attached to administrator roles don't apply to the account's root user, and a sufficiently privileged administrator could simply detach or modify the policy itself — IAM policies aren't a hard ceiling the way SCPs are, so this isn't a guarantee.
incorrect.C: Config plus SNS is a detective control: it tells the security team AFTER CloudTrail or GuardDuty has already been disabled, not a preventive guarantee that it never happens.
incorrect.D: IAM Access Analyzer identifies external/unintended access and unused permissions for review — it doesn't proactively revoke permissions in real time to block specific API calls; this describes a capability Access Analyzer doesn't have.

scenario:
A company is building a brand-new multi-account AWS environment from scratch for a 200-person engineering organization expected to grow past 500 accounts within three years. Requirements:

- Consistent security and compliance guardrails must be automatically applied to every new account from the moment it's created.
- Engineering teams need self-service account provisioning, without the central platform team manually creating each account.
- Centralized visibility into guardrail compliance status is required across all accounts.
- The platform team is only 3 people, so initial setup effort must be minimized.

Which approach best meets these requirements?

options.text: Manually create each AWS account using AWS Organizations, write custom CloudFormation StackSets to deploy baseline security resources to each account, and build an internal ticketing system for account requests.

Use AWS Control Tower to set up a landing zone with preventive (SCP-based) and detective (Config-based) guardrails, and let engineering teams self-service provision new accounts through the Account Factory, each automatically inheriting the landing zone's guardrails.
Create one large shared AWS account for all 500 engineers and use IAM policies alone to separate teams, avoiding multi-account management entirely.
Use AWS Organizations with SCPs only, and let each engineering team independently create and configure their own accounts outside any centralized governance model, retrofitting guardrails later.

explanation.correct: This is exactly what Control Tower automates: landing zone setup (Organizations plus logging/audit accounts and guardrails) with minimal manual effort for a small platform team, self-service account provisioning via Account Factory so engineering teams don't wait on the platform team, automatic application of both preventive and detective guardrails to every new account, and centralized compliance visibility through the Control Tower dashboard — satisfying every stated requirement with the least initial setup effort.

incorrect.A: Hand-building StackSets and a ticketing system for account provisioning is a large custom-engineering burden for a 3-person team — the opposite of minimizing setup effort — and doesn't give the automatic new-account guardrail application Control Tower provides out of the box.
incorrect.C: A single shared account for 500 engineers fails basic security isolation and blast-radius principles the exam consistently tests against; IAM policies alone can't replicate the isolation a multi-account strategy provides.
incorrect.D: Retrofitting guardrails after account creation, rather than applying them automatically at creation time, leaves a window of ungoverned accounts and requires ongoing manual remediation — directly contradicting "automatically applied... from the moment it's created."

scenario:
A healthcare company stores PHI in S3 buckets across multiple AWS accounts (encrypted with SSE-KMS) within the same AWS Organization. Requirements:

- A single Security account should own and manage the KMS keys used to encrypt PHI data, for centralized key management and audit.
- Application accounts must be able to use these keys to encrypt/decrypt their own S3 data, without being able to modify the key policy, delete the key, or grant other accounts access to it.
- Every key usage — every encrypt/decrypt call, by whom, from which account — must be centrally auditable.
- If a key is ever compromised or needs replacing, existing encrypted data must remain decryptable without re-encrypting the entire multi-petabyte dataset.

Which combination of actions meets these requirements?

options.text: Create a separate customer managed KMS key in each application account, and use S3 Cross-Region Replication to centralize copies of all objects in the Security account for auditing.

Create customer managed KMS keys in the Security account with a key policy that grants specific application accounts kms:Encrypt/kms:Decrypt/kms:GenerateDataKey (but not kms:PutKeyPolicy or kms:ScheduleKeyDeletion), reference the key by ARN in each application account's S3 default encryption setting, enable automatic key rotation, and rely on AWS CloudTrail (which logs all KMS API calls) for centralized auditing.
Use the AWS-managed key (aws/s3) in each application account, since AWS-managed keys already provide automatic centralized management with no configuration needed.
Create one customer managed KMS key in the Security account and share the raw key material with each application account so they can perform encryption locally without calling the KMS API each time.

explanation.correct: A KMS key policy is the primary cross-account access-control mechanism for a key: granting specific actions (Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey) to application account principals while withholding key-management actions (PutKeyPolicy, ScheduleKeyDeletion, CreateGrant) achieves centralized ownership with least-privilege cross-account usage. Every KMS API call is automatically logged to CloudTrail with the calling account and identity, satisfying centralized auditability. KMS's automatic annual key rotation replaces only the backing cryptographic material while keeping the same key ID/ARN, so previously encrypted data stays decryptable without any re-encryption — directly satisfying the compromised-key requirement.

incorrect.A: Separate keys per account plus S3 replication for "auditing" doesn't centralize key management at all (violating the core requirement) and needlessly duplicates a multi-petabyte dataset just to inspect access patterns — CloudTrail already provides this without replication.
incorrect.C: AWS-managed keys (aws/s3) cannot be shared across accounts via a custom key policy — they're scoped to a single account/service and don't support the cross-account access-control model this scenario requires, nor the fine-grained "can't delete/modify policy" separation, since there's no custom key policy to write.
incorrect.D: KMS key material is never exportable or shareable by design — that's the core security guarantee of the service, backed by HSMs. This option describes something KMS fundamentally does not support.

scenario:
A managed-service provider (MSP) manages AWS infrastructure for 50 client companies, each with its own separate AWS account (none inside the MSP's AWS Organization). MSP engineers need to operate in any client account. Requirements:

- MSP engineers must never have long-lived IAM credentials (access keys) stored in or issued for client accounts.
- Each client must be able to independently revoke the MSP's access at any time, without depending on the MSP's cooperation.
- Access granted to the MSP must be scoped to only what's needed (least privilege), and different clients may want to authorize different permission sets.
- The MSP must never be able to grant itself broader access than what each client explicitly authorized.

Which approach satisfies these requirements?

options.text: Each client creates an IAM user for the MSP with an access key and shares the credentials over a secure channel; the MSP stores these credentials in its own secrets manager.

Each client creates an IAM role with a trust policy allowing the MSP's AWS account to assume it (optionally requiring an external ID), attaches a permissions policy scoped to only what that client authorizes, and MSP engineers use AWS STS AssumeRole from their own IAM identities to obtain temporary credentials scoped to that role whenever needed.
The MSP creates a single IAM role in its own account with AdministratorAccess and shares that role's credentials with all client accounts to simplify management.
Each client adds the MSP's AWS account as a member of their AWS Organization and applies an SCP granting the MSP account full access to all resources.

explanation.correct: Cross-account IAM roles with AssumeRole are AWS's recommended pattern for third-party/MSP access: STS issues only short-lived temporary credentials on each assumption, so no long-lived credentials are ever created or shared. The client fully owns the role and its trust policy, so access can be revoked instantly (by deleting or updating the role) without the MSP's involvement. The client also defines the role's permissions policy, so each client can scope different permissions, and the MSP can never grant itself more than that policy allows since the client — not the MSP — controls it. An external ID in the trust policy additionally mitigates the confused-deputy problem when one MSP account is trusted by many clients.

incorrect.A: Long-lived IAM access keys directly violate "must never have long-lived credentials," are harder to revoke instantly compared to updating a role's trust policy, and carry ongoing credential-leakage risk.
incorrect.C: A single AdministratorAccess role fully controlled by the MSP violates least privilege and "must never grant itself broader access than authorized" — the MSP, not each client, would control the permissions, and every client would receive identical, excessive access.
incorrect.D: Making the MSP a full member of each client's Organization with a broad SCP is both operationally extreme and gives the MSP far more integration and visibility into the client's environment than a scoped cross-account role requires. SCPs also don't function as a mechanism to grant access to an external account this way — they set maximum permissions within an Organization, not cross-account grants.
